coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of 17 β -estradiol electrochemical sensor using polyaniline/carbon dot-coated glassy carbon electrode with synergistically enhanced electrochemical stability
coredata.dc:creator: Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Alvior Sinoy, Ma Concepcion
de Luna, Mark Daniel G.
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Previous 17 β -estradiol sensors required expensive reagents or complicated fabrication of sensing probes. In this work, a cheap, simple, and reusable electrochemical sensor based on commercially available polyaniline (PANI) and carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from iota-carrageenan was developed for the sensitive detection of 17 β -estradiol. The sensor was simply prepared by drop-casting CDs/PANI composite on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) using poly(vinylidene fluoride) as a binder. With synergistic contributions from both CDs and PANI, the CDs-PANI/GCE was much more electrochemically stable than the CDs/GCE or PANI/GCE. The CDs-PANI/GCE was sensitive to 17 β -estradiol across a linear range from 0.001 to 100 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 43 nmol L−1. The electrochemical measurement can be performed in 2 min and the probe can be reused for several hundred times. The CDs-PANI/GCE was selective towards 17 β -estradiol against several interferences and gave excellent recovery between 94.4 and 103.7 % from real sample analysis. From intensive investigation on electron transfer process and energy levels, the oxidation reaction of 17 β -estradiol occurred on the surface of CDs-PANI/GCE via favorable energy levels and dominantly surface adsorption process through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding between 17 β -estradiol and CDs/PANI. Such unique interfacial interactions also resulted in the synergistically enhanced electrochemical stability of the modified electrode.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of 17 β -estradiol electrochemical sensor using polyaniline/carbon dot-coated glassy carbon electrode with synergistically enhanced electrochemical stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120 Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120 Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, 1101, Quezon City, Philippines
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, 1101, Quezon City, Philippines

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Previous 17 β -estradiol sensors required expensive reagents or complicated fabrication of sensing probes. In this work, a cheap, simple, and reusable electrochemical sensor based on commercially available polyaniline (PANI) and carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from iota-carrageenan was developed for the sensitive detection of 17 β -estradiol. The sensor was simply prepared by drop-casting CDs/PANI composite on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) using poly(vinylidene fluoride) as a binder. With synergistic contributions from both CDs and PANI, the CDs-PANI/GCE was much more electrochemically stable than the CDs/GCE or PANI/GCE. The CDs-PANI/GCE was sensitive to 17 β -estradiol across a linear range from 0.001 to 100 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 43 nmol L−1. The electrochemical measurement can be performed in 2 min and the probe can be reused for several hundred times. The CDs-PANI/GCE was selective towards 17 β -estradiol against several interferences and gave excellent recovery between 94.4 and 103.7 % from real sample analysis. From intensive investigation on electron transfer process and energy levels, the oxidation reaction of 17 β -estradiol occurred on the surface of CDs-PANI/GCE via favorable energy levels and dominantly surface adsorption process through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding between 17 β -estradiol and CDs/PANI. Such unique interfacial interactions also resulted in the synergistically enhanced electrochemical stability of the modified electrode.

ce:section.ce:para: Estrogen is a steroid hormone directly responsible for development and regulation of female reproductive and fertility systems [1]. It is also an indicator of sexual maturation status and several diseases [2]. The estrogen level in women is related to lung, ovarian, and uterine cancers. It can affect weight gain, depression, fatigue, mood swing, and trouble sleeping, etc. [3] There are three major compounds contributing to estrogenic hormonal activity, including estrone (E1), 17 β -estradiol (E2), and estriol (E3). 17 β -estradiol is the major female sex hormone [4]. It is responsible for the regulation of estrous and menstrual female reproductive cycles and widely used for growth and milk yield promotion in animals [5]. A large quantity of 17 β -estradiol secreted from human and animal urine then contaminates water resources and water animals. Continuous consumption of water animals as foods then leads to a high intake of 17 β -estradiol, which can disturb the endocrine systems [6]. As a result, 17 β -estradiol is an emerging contaminant and has become a global problem [7]. Hence, the detection of 17 β -estradiol is important, regarding to these health and environmental issues.

Various methods have been developed for the detection of 17 β -estradiol, including high-performance liquid chromatography [8], chemiluminescence immunoassay [9], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent immunoassay [10]. However, these techniques have several drawbacks owing to expensive apparatus and reagents, time-consuming procedure, and impracticality for on-site testing because of large size of instruments [11]. Recently, electrochemical techniques have received great attention due to several advantages, such as high sensitivity and relatively cheap and portable instruments, which are convenient for on-site testing [12]. Electrochemical methods have been developed for the detection of 17 β -estradiol. A number of nanomaterials have been used as electrode materials for 17 β -estradiol electrochemical analysis because of their advantages in small size, large surface area, and high electrocatalytic activity [13]. N. Chaisuwan et al. reported the use of CdSe quantum dots for the determination of 17 β -estradiol using differential pulse anodic stripping voltammetry, yielding a detection limit of 50 pg mL−1 [14]. M. Arvand et al. used graphene quantum dots/electrochemically synthesized poly(sulfosalicylic acid) for the detection of 17 β -estradiol and progesterone via linear sweep voltammetry, yielding detection limits of 0.23 and 0.31 nmol L−1, respectively [15]. However, the above-mentioned sensing nanomaterials are either toxic, expensive, or difficult to prepare and the electrochemical probes often required several steps of fabrication. Therefore, the practical, simple-to-prepare nanomaterials and electrochemical sensors for the highly sensitive detection of 17 β -estradiol are still desired.

Carbon dots (CDs) are attractive nanomaterials with unique properties, for example, surface-sensitive properties, high chemical stability, and aqueous solubility [16,17]. Various functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups, and conjugated carbon core can interact with analyte molecules, such as ions, organic molecules, biomolecules, nanoparticles, and polymers via coordinate covalent bonding, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic, and π-π stacking interactions [18,19]. These interfacial interactions lead to a change in physical and electrochemical properties of CDs, allowing them to be used as sensing probes [20]. CDs have been applied as electrochemical probes with high sensitivity for the detection of various analytes, for instance, adrenaline [21], dopamine [22], capsaicin [23], paracetamol [24], bisphenol A [25], and amoxicillin [26]. Interestingly, CDs have been used for the detection of 17 β -estradiol. M. Zaid et al. reported the use of CDs-modified screen-printed carbon electrode functionalized with aptamer for 17 β -estradiol detection, yielding a detection limit of 0.5 × 10−12 mol L−1. Aptamers are capable of interacting specifically with the desired targets; however, they are expensive and quickly degrade [27]. Therefore, the search for alternative materials to be used in conjunction with CDs for 17 β -estradiol detection is still needed.

Recently, conducting polymers have received great attention in electrochemical sensing due to their excellent conductivity, straightforward fabrication, and direct electrical readout for target analytes with high sensitivity and selectivity [28]. Among well-known conducting polymers, polyaniline (PANI) has been widely utilized as an electrode material because of its promising properties in terms of high conductivity, high solubility, and low cost [29]. Therefore, combining extraordinary properties from CDs and PANI can provide high conductivity, excellent stability, and good selectivity for unique sensitivity towards 17 β -estradiol. Although CDs/PANI composites have been used in electrochemical sensors for several analytes [30,31], to the best of our knowledge, their use for the 17 β -estradiol detection has not been reported. Furthermore, the previously reported CDs/PANI sensors for other analytes mostly involved multi-step fabrication methods or complicated strategies, such as fabrication of nanofibers, electrochemical polymerization, and the use of additional active materials [30,32,33]. Herein, an electrochemical sensor for the detection of 17 β -estradiol was developed by simply drop-casting both CDs and commercially available PANI on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (Fig. 1 ). Mimicking electrodes in energy storage devices, poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), a widely used binder, was used to provide mechanical integrity of the coating layer. The CDs were synthesized from iota-carrageenan using a one-step hydrothermal process [23]. The electrochemical detection of 17 β -estradiol using CDs-PANI/GCE was recorded using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and investigated in terms of scan rate, pH, accumulation time, and 17 β -estradiol concentration. The reusability of the fabricated electrode and the detection of 17 β -estradiol in different types of water and human serum sample were also studied. In summary, this paper reports on a novel and simple strategy to prepare an electrochemical sensor based on CDs and PANI for the rapid detection of 17 β -estradiol with excellent selectivity, sensitivity, stability, and reusability, indicating high potential and practical applications for medical diagnosis, environmental control, and pharmaceutical formulation.

All chemicals were of at least analytical grade. 17 β -estradiol, polyaniline (emeraldine salt, an average molecular weight > 15,000 Da), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), phosphate-buffered saline (10X PBS), N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), human serum (male AB plasma), and PVDF were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetonitrile (HPLC grade) and ethanol were obtained from Carlo Erba. CaCl2, Cd(NO3)2, CoCl2, FeCl3, KCl, LiCl, MgCl2, Na2SO4, PbCl2, SnCl2, ZnCl2, l-ascorbic acid, citric acid, testosterone, and urea (https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Drinking water was purchased from a supermarket and tap water and river water were taken at Thammasat University. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Model CHI 104, 3 mm in diameter) was bought from GuangYi Store, China. A 17 β -estradiol stock solution was prepared in ethanol while others were in deionized (DI) water (resistivity > 18 MΩ cm).

CDs were synthesized according to the method reported in our publication [23]. Briefly, iota-carrageenan was mixed with H2O2 (30 % v/v) and then heated at 220 °C for 12 h in a Teflon-line autoclave. The mixture was filtrated, neutralized using NaOH, and dialyzed for 72 h. The brown solid CDs were obtained after freeze-drying. Detailed characterizations of the CDs can be found in our previous publication [23].

A bare GCE was first polished on 0.05 μm alumina powder, rinsed with DI water, and ultrasonicated in DI water and ethanol for 5 min each to obtain a mirror-like surface. CDs and PANI were mixed thoroughly in a mortar with various weight ratios of 2:6, 3.2:4.8, 4.8:3.2, and 6:2, which are named as 25CDs-PANI/GCE, 40CDs-PANI/GCE, 60CDs-PANI/GCE, and 75CDs-PANI/GCE, respectively. GCE electrodes with only PANI and CDs were also prepared and denoted as PANI/GCE and CDs/GCE, respectively. PVDF (20 % w/w) as a binder was mixed with the composites. The total weight was fixed at 3 mg. NMP (100 μL) was added into the mixture, which was then ultrasonicated for 30 min. The modified electrode was then prepared by drop-casting the mixture (20 μL) on the surface of GCE and drying at 110 °C for 3 h in an oven before use. Surface topography of the modified electrode was characterized using field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM7800F) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, Oxford X-Max 20).

All electrochemical measurements were performed using a potentiostat (Metrohm Autolab, PGSTAT204). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were recorded with a conventional three-electrode system at room temperature. The bare GCE and the modified GCE were used as the working electrodes with a platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (3 M NaCl) as the reference electrode. PBS solution (1X) was used as an electrolyte. A 17 β -estradiol stock solution (200 μmol L−1) was prepared in ethanol and mixed with the PBS solution and the pH was adjusted using HCl. The CV and LSV were recorded using a potential range from 0.0 to 1.4 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 after the accumulation time of 60 s. Determination of the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO and LUMO) energy states of CDs was performed using acetonitrile (4 mL) containing 0.1 M tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate as the electrolyte at a potential range from −1.45 to 1.55 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and interference studies
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method, human serum sample and different types of water: drinking water, tap water, and river water were used for real sample analysis. The water samples and human serum sample (10 mL) were filtered through a filter paper and 0.22 μm nylon membrane and then mixed with the PBS solution (1X, 10 mL). The samples were spiked with known concentrations of standard 17 β -estradiol. All of the samples were filtered through the 0.22 μm nylon membrane and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator before use. The amounts of 17 β -estradiol were determined by electrochemical measurement and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

The amounts of 17 β -estradiol in standard solutions and real samples were analyzed by HPLC (Shimadzu, LC-20AT series) equipped with a UV detector (220 nm). The samples were filtered through the 0.22 μm nylon membrane. The C18 column (4.6 mm i.d. × 150 mm, 5 μm particle diameter) joined with a guard column (4.6 mm i.d. × 12.5 mm, 5 μm particle diameter) and the mobile phase (acetonitrile and DI water, 70/30 % v/v) were used. The injection volume was 10 μL with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 and 5 min of the retention time. All measurements were performed at ambient temperature (ca. 25 ± 1 °C).

The selectivity of the CDs-PANI/GCE for the detection of 17 β -estradiol was investigated using Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, ascorbic acid, citric acid, testosterone, and urea as interferences. Each interference in PBS solution (100 μmol L−1) was mixed with 17 β -estradiol (100 μmol L−1) and transferred into the electrochemical cell. A 17 β -estradiol solution without adding interferences was prepared as a control sample.

PANI and CDs were chosen as electroactive materials and PVDF as a binder for providing adhesion of the composite materials on the GCE surface was fixed at 20 % by weight. Elemental mapping of nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine was investigated using SEM-EDS as these elements are indicators of PANI, CDs, and PVDF, respectively (Fig. 2 ). The SEM-EDS images of the modified electrode showed that the PANI, CDs, and PVDF distributed relatively homogeneously throughout the film. Different weight ratios between PANI and CDs were then investigated to explore the sensing performance for the 17 β -estradiol detection. All modified electrodes were immersed in 100 μmol L−1 17 β -estradiol-containing PBS solution at pH 1 as electrolyte and the LSV measurement was carried out. The PANI/GCE provided the highest oxidation current response in the first scan, followed by 25CDs-PANI/GCE, 40CDs-PANI/GCE, 60CDs-PANI/GCE, 75CDs-PANI/GCE, and CDs/GCE, respectively (Fig. 3 a–f). However, after seven scans the current response of PANI/GCE dramatically decreased. On the other hand, the current responses of the modified GCE containing CDs, especially, 25CDs-PANI/GCE and 40CDs-PANI/GCE, only slightly reduced. The rapid decay in current response of PANI/GCE was due to the hydrolytic degradation of PANI (Fig. 3g). Water molecules in the electrolyte attacked the electronically electropositive carbon atoms of PANI. As a result of the hydrolysis, the polymer chains degraded, leading to the conductivity fading and reduction in current response [34]. The enhanced stability of CDs-PANI/GCE was possibly attributed to the π-π stacking interaction between the CC bonds from CDs and the conjugated PANI backbone and hydrogen bonding/electrostatic interactions between hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs and –NH, and –NH+ groups from PANI. These favorable interactions improved the chemical and physical stability of the electroactive layer and reduced the hydrolysis of PANI. However, higher amounts of CDs in the modified GCE gave lower oxidation current stability because high aqueous solubility of CDs led to desorption of the active layer from the GCE surface (Fig. 3g). From these results, only PANI or CDs alone did not act as good electrode materials. It can be concluded that a combination of PANI and CDs with an optimal ratio synergistically yielded high conductivity and stability as the composite electrode materials. Therefore, the 25CDs-PANI/GCE was chosen for all subsequent experiments because it simultaneously gave high current response and the highest stability.

The electrochemical analysis of 17 β -estradiol was performed using 25CDs-PANI/GCE in PBS solution containing 100 μmol L−1 17 β -estradiol. The CV experiment was carried out using a potential voltage between 0.0 and 1.4 V and a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The CV curve of 25CDs-PANI/GCE showed the oxidation peak at 0.85 V as shown in Fig. 4 a, indicating that 17 β -estradiol was oxidized by the CDs-PANI modified electrode. The reduction peak was not observed, confirming that this is an irreversible process. The oxidation reaction took place at the hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring of 17 β -estradiol molecule, which was then converted to the carbonyl group, led to an electron transfer to the electrode, and consequently the enhanced current response (Fig. 4b) [15,35]. As a control experiment, the 25CDs-PANI/GCE in the absence of 17 β -estradiol exhibited a much lower current response and these results thus confirm that the current response from the blue curve came from the oxidation of 17 β -estradiol. To further confirm the sensitivity of 25CDs-PANI/GCE towards 17 β -estradiol, the current response of the bare GCE as another control in the presence and absence of 17 β -estradiol was measured. The results showed no oxidation peak current in both cases. Based on these results, the 25CDs-PANI/GCE could sense 17 β -estradiol via the electron transfer process from the oxidation reaction.

The effects of scan rate on the electrochemical properties can be used to examine various characteristics of the electron transfer process and electrochemical sensing mechanism. Since the oxidation of 17 β -estradiol is the irreversible process, the LSV measurement was sufficient for use and the scan rate was varied from 10 to 100 mV s−1. The oxidation peak potential shifted to higher potentials with increasing scan rate, indicating that the electrochemical oxidation reaction is kinetically limited by the ion adsorption and diffusion to the surface of the electrode (Fig. 5 a) [36]. The effects of scan rate on the oxidation current response were then examined to determine whether the interaction between 25CDs-PANI/GCE and 17 β -estradiol was via an adsorption or a diffusion-controlled process. Based on the theory of the surface adsorption process, the current responses and scan rates were plotted according to Eq. (1) (Fig. 5b) [37]:

Both coefficients of determination (R2) were high, which indicates that the sensing of 17 β -estradiol by 25CDs-PANI/GCE was governed by both surface adsorption and diffusion-controlled processes. However, the R2 value from the Fig. 5b is higher than that of Fig. 5c, indicating that the surface adsorption model gives a better fit than the diffusion one, and therefore, the adsorption is more dominant than the diffusion.

To further confirm that the adsorption process was more dominant in controlling the 17 β -estradiol sensing, a plot of logarithm of current response versus logarithm of scan rate was constructed. The slopes of 0.5 and 1.0 are the ideal values for the diffusion- and adsorption-controlled processes, respectively [38]. The fitting curve was found to be linear: logi
p = 0.8165logV – 1.981, R2 = 0.9915 (Fig. 6 a). Indeed, a slope of 0.8165 is closer to 1.0 than 0.5, and therefore, it can be concluded that the adsorption of 17 β -estradiol is more dominant than the diffusion in controlling the electron transfer and the sensing of 17 β -estradiol by the 25CDs-PANI/GCE. The dominant adsorption process was possibly due to the strong interactions between 17 β -estradiol and electrode materials through π-π and electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding.

To further examine the electron transfer process between 17β-estradiol and 25CDs-PANI/GCE, the number of transferred electrons involved in the electrochemical oxidation was calculated using the Laviron's equation (Eq. (3)) [39]:

Since the value of α was assumed to be 0.5 for the irreversible process [40], n was calculated to be 0.94. Therefore, the oxidation reaction of 17 β -estradiol by 25CDs-PANI/GCE is a one-electron transfer process, which is consistent with the previous reports [15,35].

According to the shift in oxidation peak potential with changing the scan rate, the electron transfer between 17β-estradiol and 25CDs-PANI/GCE was whether a quasi-irreversible or an irreversible process. where α is the electron transfer coefficient, n is the number of electrons transferred, k

The %Real value was found to be 4338 %, indicating that the electroactive area is more than 43 times larger than the geometrical area of the electrode. This means that the CDs and PANI composites were extremely active towards 17β-estradiol oxidation due to a high surface area provided by the nanosized CDs and synergistically electrochemical contributions from both CDs and PANI.

The effects of pH on the current response and peak potential of the 17 β -estradiol oxidation by 25CDs-PANI/GCE were investigated (Fig. 6c). The oxidation current response of 17 β -estradiol decreased with increasing pH. The pH above 6 produced negligible current responses, indicating that acidic conditions are necessary for the electrochemical oxidation of 17 β -estradiol and the concentration of hydrogen ion (H+) controls the reaction. The pH of 1 gave the highest current response because of the highest concentration of hydrogen ion, providing the highest conductivity among the tested electrolytes [45]. To find the number of electrons and protons involved in the electrochemical process, the relationship between the oxidation current peak potential (E
pa ) and pH was investigated (Fig. 6d). The oxidation peak potential was pH-dependent and gradually shifted to lower values by decreasing solution acidity from pH 1 to 5, indicating that the hydrogen ions took part in the oxidation reaction [40]. The curve between E
pa and pH was found to be linear with a slope of −0.0586 V, which is close to a theoretical value of −0.0592 V in the Nernst equation (Eq. (8)):
(8)

0 ′
=
E 0
− 0.0592 p H
where
E
0 ′
is the apparent formal potential and
E 0
is the formal potential. Therefore, the results confirm the same number of electron and proton in the electrochemical oxidation of 17 β -estradiol on the 25CDs-PANI/GCE [46,47], which is consistent with the previous reports [15,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of energy levels and sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
From the analysis above, it can be concluded that the 17 β -estradiol sensing by 25CDs-PANI/GCE occurred via the dominantly adsorption-controlled process and quasi-irreversible one-electron/one-proton oxidation reaction. We believed that the strong interactions between 17 β -estradiol and 25-CDs-PANI/GCE through hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking led to the adsorption of 17 β -estradiol on the electrode surface (Fig. 7 a), leading to an oxidation reaction and interfacial electron transfer, because the bare GCE did not produce a signal (Fig. 7b). In addition to the adsorption process, energy levels of the active materials must also be favorable for an efficient electron transfer process. To further rationalize the sensing of 17 β -estradiol, the energy levels 17 β -estradiol, CDs, and PANI were evaluated. Based on previous reports, the highest-occupied and lowest-unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of PANI (−5.74 eV and −3.39 eV) [48] and estradiol (−5.61 eV and 0.22 eV) [49] were used for the investigation. To determine HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the CDs, the CDs solution in NMP (6 % w/v) was drop-cast on the polished GCE and the CV measurement was carried out. The HOMO level of CDs was calculated based on Eqs. (9) and (10) [50]:
(9)
E
HOMO = -e (E
ox. vs. NHE + 4.75) (eV)
(10)
E
ox.
vs NHE = E
ox. vs Ag/AgCl + 0.197 (eV)
(11)
E
LUMO = E
HOMO + E
g (eV)
where E
ox. vs NHE and E
ox. vs Ag/AgCl are the oxidation peak potentials of CDs with respect to normal hydrogen and Ag/AgCl reference electrodes, respectively. From the CV curve, the E
ox.
vs Ag/AgCl was determined to be 0.86 V (Fig. 7b). The E
HOMO was then calculated to be −5.81 eV. The LUMO level (E
LUMO ) could not be obtained due to the quasi-irreversible oxidation of CDs. Therefore, the E
LUMO was determined from the E
HOMO and an optical band gap (E
g ) using Eq. (11). The optical band gap was calculated to be 3.67 eV using the UV–vis absorption [23] and the Tauc plot by plotting (
α h v )
r
versus
h v
, where
α ,
h ,
and
v
are the adsorption coefficient of the CDs, Planck's constant, and light frequency, respectively, and
r
is 2 for a direct band gap material [51] (Fig. 7c). Therefore, the E
LUMO of CDs was then calculated to be −2.14 eV. As shown in Fig. 7d, when a positive voltage was applied to the system, the potential energy of the electrode dropped. Then, the electrons were transferred from CDs and PANI to the GCE (step 1). The HOMO levels of the CDs, PANI, and estradiol are well-matched to facilitate the electron transfer from 17 β -estradiol to the electron-deficient CDs and PANI (step 2), thereby leading to the oxidation of 17β-estradiol. It can be concluded that CDs and PANI synergistically provided strong interactions and surface adsorption of 17 β -estradiol and their matched energy levels led to the efficient interfacial electron transfer process and unique sensing properties.

The analytical performance of 25CDs-PANI/GCE on different 17 β -estradiol concentrations was examined. Nine different concentrations of 17 β -estradiol (0.001–200 μmol L−1) in PBS at pH 1 were used. The oxidation current responses were found to increase with increasing 17 β -estradiol concentration (Fig. 8 a). The plot of current responses and concentrations was further investigated (Fig. 8b). The results showed that the electrochemical sensor had a linear response to 17 β -estradiol concentration from 0.001 to 100 μmol L−1 (inset of Fig. 8b). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 43 nmol L−1 using 3
σ / slope
, where
σ
is the standard deviation of the peak current. Our linear range is one of the largest and LOD is one of the lowest as compared to those in previous reports (Table 1 ). The GQDs/PSSA/GO/GCE [15], RGO/CuTthP/GCE [52], and Ru(dcbpy)3Cl2-NCQDs/GCE [53] exhibited lower LODs, but with the narrower linear ranges. The Pol/HRP/Pt electrode showed a wide linear range from 100 to 200,000 nmol L−1, but the LOD was 105 nmol L−1, which is larger than our LOD [54]. M.H. Mat Zaid et al. used aptamer/CDs/SPE [55] for 17 β -estradiol detection and their LOD was among the best. However, the linear range was narrow and their electrodes involved the use of aptamer, which was difficult to prepare and is generally unstable in harsh working conditions. Our modified electrode, on the other hand, gave a combination of wide linear range and low LOD and can be easily prepared by the drop-casting method with neither additional functionalization nor the use of expensive or unstable materials. Therefore, the CDs-PANI/GCE developed in this work is considered as one of the most practical sensors for 17 β -estradiol detection reported to date.

As the current response was sensitive to the scan rate and the sensing occurred through adsorption and electron transfer process, it can be expected that the sensitivity, i.e. LOD, is dependent upon the scan rate. To the best of our knowledge, the scan rate dependence of LOD has not been reported. Therefore, in this experiment, the LSV curves were measured at different scan rates using different concentrations of 17 β -estradiol. Although all scan rates produced linear relationship with the 17 β -estradiol concentration, the slopes were different (Fig. 8c). The difference in slope is equivalent to the difference in LODs. It was found that the lowest LOD value was obtained to be 43 nmol L−1 at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 whereas a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 yielded an LOD of more than five times higher (Fig. 8d). A further increase in scan rate higher than 100 mV s−1 adversely increased the LODs. A scan rate of 100 mV s−1 was the best possibly because it allowed the most efficient surface adsorption of 17 β -estradiol and interfacial electron transfer for the best sensitivity. Low scan rates might lead to saturation of analyte on the electrode surface while high scan rates might not allow the analyte to sufficiently interact with the electrode. This proves that for the development of any electrochemical sensors, the scan rate should be optimized to achieve the best LOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of 17 β -estradiol in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the practical applicability and feasibility of the proposed method for the 17 β -estradiol detection, human serum sample and different types of water: drinking water, tap water, and river water, were chosen as real samples for quantitative analysis. Each real sample was spiked with three different concentrations of the 17 β -estradiol standard solutions (10.0, 25.0, and 50.0 μmol L−1), which are within the linear range. The concentrations of 17 β -estradiol in real samples were then determined using HPLC and LSV methods. From the HPLC chromatograms, the signal of 17 β -estradiol from the standard solutions showed up at 2.1 min (Fig. 9 ). All 17 β -estradiol-spiked real samples also showed signals at 2.1 min, indicating that 17 β -estradiol was successfully extracted from the real samples. The percentage recoveries of 17 β -estradiol in these real samples ranged from 94.4 to 103.7 % and 97.4–102.4 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 2.9 % and 2.0 % for the LSV and HPLC methods, respectively ( Table 2). The t-test and F-test were performed and it was found that there were no significant differences between both tests at a 95 % confident level. Therefore, it can be concluded that the electrochemical sensor developed in this work was selective to 17 β -estradiol, gave the detection results as accurate as the conventional HPLC method, and is therefore suitable for practical applications.

To investigate the selectivity of the 25CDs-PANI/GCE for the detection of 17 β -estradiol, several interference ions and compounds were added to the 17 β -estradiol solutions and the LSV curves were measured and compared with that of the pure 17 β -estradiol solution. The following interference ions and compounds were used: Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, ascorbic acid, citric acid, testosterone, and urea because they are ubiquitous. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the oxidation current responses were not significantly affected by the interferences. The relative current responses in the presence of interferences were in between 93.2 and 101.5 %, compared with the control which is the pure 17 β -estradiol solution (Fig. 10b). In addition to the real sample analysis, these results confirmed that 25CDs-PANI/GCE was selective to 17 β -estradiol in the presence of interferences.

The effects of accumulation time on 17 β -estradiol sensing were investigated. In this experiment, the 25CDs-PANI/GCE was immersed in the PBS containing 100 μmol L−1 17 β -estradiol at different periods of time before the LSV measurement was performed. The oxidation current response slightly increased with an increase in the accumulation time from 0 to 2 min and then remained steady (Fig. 11 a). This result indicated that the measurement could give the best results within 2 min, which are much faster than other previously reported methods, such as gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, and mass spectrometry, which usually need at least 5 min of measurement time, excluding sample preparation and instrument calibration [8]. Furthermore, the reusability of the modified electrode was studied by recording repetitive LSV scans. After 70 successive scans, the current response reduced to 81.7 % with respect to the initial response and then to 64.7 % after 100 successive scans (Fig. 11b). We suspected that the decrease in current response was due to the depletion of 17 β -estradiol in the electrolyte as the oxidation reaction of 17 β -estradiol is an quasi-irreversible process. To verify that the decrease in current response was due to the depletion of 17 β -estradiol, we replaced the 17 β -estradiol-containing electrolyte with the fresh one in every 10 scans. The peak current remained above 83.2 % and 65.4 % after 170 and 280 successive scans, respectively (Fig. 11c and d). In other words, the recovery of the LSV signals was much improved after the freshly made electrolyte was used. Therefore, the results suggested that our 25CDs-PANI/GCE can be used as a rapid sensing system and reused for at least 280 scans with sufficient signal retention. These results also demonstrated the extraordinary electrochemical stability of the modified electrode. To the best of our knowledge, the reusability of the electrode for 17 β -estradiol electrochemical detection has never been reported to date and this is the first report to investigate the reusability of the modified electrode for 17 β -estradiol sensing. The superior stability of the CDs-PANI/GCE electrode is attributed to the strong interactions between CDs and PANI and the use of PVDF binder.

In this work, an electrochemical probe was developed based on CDs and PANI as the electroactive materials and PVDF as the binder for the detection of 17 β -estradiol. The CDs-PANI/GCE sensor was prepared by a simple drop-casting method. The synergistic combination of their properties yielded high sensitivity, selectivity, accuracy, reusability, and rapid detection for 17 β -estradiol sensing. The oxidation reaction of 17 β -estradiol and interfacial electron transfer occurred on the surface of CDs-PANI/GCE via favorable energy levels and dominantly adsorption-controlled process through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding between 17 β -estradiol and CDs/PANI. The 25CDs-PANI/GCE exhibited the highest oxidation current response of 17 β -estradiol in PBS at pH 1, which was sensitive across the linear range from 0.001 to 100 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 43 nmol L−1. The detection limit was found to be scan-rate dependence. The detection results could also be obtained within 2 min, suitable for a rapid, real-time detection. The 25CDs-PANI/GCE can also be reused for at least 280 times with sufficient signal retention and exhibited high selectivity to 17 β -estradiol in the presence of several interferences. Furthermore, it could determine 17 β -estradiol in real samples with excellent accuracy. In a brief conclusion, the high-performance electrochemical probe developed in this work is one of the most practical sensors for the 17 β -estradiol detection.
